frontmatter.title: Linear Mixed-Effects Models: Why We Need Them

content.name: Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial 4.0 International

children.value: Project Leader & Instructor, Computational Behavioral Sciences, LS100, FAS, Harvard University
In this notebook, we will analyze data from
doing a repetitive exercise (e.g., push-ups) in
In each session, the athlete performs many
Do athletes show lower correctness scores in the session with a higher number of repeats?
(i.e., does performance decrease when the volume of work is higher?)
Why a Simple t-test Is Not Enough

children.label: Why a Simple t-test Is Not Enough

children.value: At first glance, this might look like a simple comparison of Session 1 vs. Session 2 correctness scores. However, there is an important complication:
The data are clustered within athletes.
are likely more similar to each other than to repeats from another athlete.
of simple tests like the standard t-test or simple linear regression.
If we ignore the fact that multiple measurements come from the same person, we:
Over-count evidence (treat each repeat as if it were a completely independent person).
(finding “significant” effects that are not actually reliable).
To handle this structure properly, we need a model that:
each athlete has their own baseline performance
What Is a Linear Mixed-Effects Model?

children.label: What Is a Linear Mixed-Effects Model?

children.value: is like a regular linear regression model, but with two types of effects:
These are the effects we are primarily interested in.
Example: “On average, correctness is 0.08 lower in the high-volume session.”
In our case, each athlete may have a different baseline correctness level.
Example: Athlete A is generally more accurate than Athlete B, regardless of session.
The random effects allow the model to say:
“All athletes share some common pattern (fixed effect), but each athlete is allowed to start at their own baseline level (random effect).”
while still respecting that they are different.
The random effects (which capture group-level variation) are assumed to follow a normal distribution with a mean of (0).
The errors or residuals (the differences between the observed and predicted values) are assumed to be normally distributed with a mean of (0) and a constant variance.
There is a linear relationship between the predictors and the response variable.
The variance of the errors is constant across all levels of the predictor variables (homoscedasticity).
Our Model for the Push-Up Scenario

children.label: Our Model for the Push-Up Scenario

children.value: We will model the correctness score for each repeat using a simple mixed-effects structure.

β1\beta_1β1​

children.value: “After accounting for differences between athletes, is correctness lower in the high-volume session?”

children.html: u0iu_{0i}u0i​

children.value: “Some athletes are generally more accurate; some are less accurate. We let the model learn that instead of forcing everyone to be identical.”
of the data: repeats are nested inside sessions, and sessions are nested inside athletes.
Simulating Data for 4 Athletes and 2 Sessions

children.label: Simulating Data for 4 Athletes and 2 Sessions

children.value: We will simulate a small dataset to match our scenario:
on correctness (to mimic fatigue), the same on average for all athletes.
There is some random trial-to-trial noise.
This will give us a realistic dataset to fit our linear mixed-effects model.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# For reproducibility
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# Named athletes
athletes = ["susan_01", "alex_02", "maya_03", "david_04"]

# Named sessions
sessions = ["session_20", "session_30"]  # 20 repeats vs 30 repeats
n_repeats = {"session_20": 20, "session_30": 30}

# Fixed effect: average change in correctness in the high-volume session
beta_session_high = -0.08  # on average, correctness drops by 0.08 in session_30

rows = []

for athlete in athletes:
    # Random intercept: each athlete has their own baseline correctness
    baseline_correctness = rng.normal(loc=0.85, scale=0.05)
    
    for session in sessions:
        # Indicator: 0 for low volume (session_20), 1 for high volume (session_30)
        session_high = 1 if session == "session_30" else 0
        
        # Number of repeats in this session
        n = n_repeats[session]
        
        for repeat in range(1, n + 1):
            # Mean correctness for this athlete & session
            mu = baseline_correctness + beta_session_high * session_high
            
            # Add trial-to-trial noise
            correctness = rng.normal(loc=mu, scale=0.05)
            
            # Clip to [0, 1] to keep it a valid score
            correctness = np.clip(correctness, 0, 1)
            
            rows.append({
                "athlete_id": athlete,
                "session_label": session,   # "session_20" or "session_30"
                "session_high": session_high,  # 0 = low volume, 1 = high volume
                "repeat_id": repeat,
                "correctness": correctness
            })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

children.value: Fitting a Linear Mixed-Effects Model in Python

children.label: Fitting a Linear Mixed-Effects Model in Python

In Python, we can fit this model using the
After fitting the model, we will inspect the summary table to address our main questions:

We will fit the model and then inspect the summary table for the fixed effects and random effects variances (

children.value:
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

# Fit a linear mixed-effects model:
# correctness ~ session_high  with random intercepts for athlete_id
model = smf.mixedlm(
    formula="correctness ~ session_high",
    data=df,
    groups=df["athlete_id"]
) # you can also add method = "lbfgs"/ "nm", etc. inside the mixedlm() function.

result = model.fit()
print(result.summary())

# For quick intuition: mean correctness by session_label
print("\nMean correctness by session_label:")
print(df.groupby("session_label")["correctness"].mean())

children.value: ), i.e., the variability of the correctness scores
Even after accounting for session and athlete, there is still some trial-to-trial variability in correctness.
The model assumes this residual noise has variance ≈ 0.0019.
how well the model fits the data
(higher is better, for the same data and model class).
On its own, this number is mostly useful when you:
(e.g., with and without a random effect, or with and without an extra fixed effect), using criteria like AIC or LRT (Likelihood Ratio Test).
For our purposes in this teaching example, you can treat it as a model-fit statistic that we don’t interpret directly in “correctness units”.
Indicates whether the numerical optimization algorithm successfully found a set of parameter values that maximize the likelihood.
The model fitting procedure succeeded, and the results are trustworthy (at least numerically).
10. Putting It All Together (Plain Language Summary)

children.label: 10. Putting It All Together (Plain Language Summary)

children.value: From this model, we can say:
, so we can trust the numerical estimates.
So, in the context of our question:
Yes, the model provides strong evidence that correctness scores are lower in the session with more repeats (
), even after accounting for differences between athletes.
If you’d like to go deeper into linear mixed-effects models, these resources are a great next step:
Introduction to Linear Mixed Models (UCLA OARC)
A very accessible, concept-focused introduction to linear mixed models with examples across different software.
A Beginner’s Introduction to Mixed Effects Models (Meghan Hall)
A gentle, blog-style introduction that clearly explains fixed vs random effects, with intuitive examples and visuals—excellent for building intuition.
Using the linear mixed model to analyze nonnormal data distributions in longitudinal designs
Barr, D. J., Levy, R., Scheepers, C., & Tily, H. J. (2013).
Discusses why and how to use linear mixed models in realistic experimental settings, especially with non-normal data and repeated measures.
Linear Mixed Effects Models – statsmodels Documentation
Official documentation showing how to fit and interpret linear mixed-effects models in Python using
